elements.sent-2411-001: Achieving a flexible and efficient sharing of wireless resources among a wide range of novel applications and services is one of the major goals of \gls{6g .}
elements.sent-2411-002: Accordingly, this work investigates the performance of a real-time system that coexists with a broadband service in a frame-based wireless channel.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, we consider real-time remote tracking of an information source, where a device monitors its evolution and sends updates to a \gls{bs , which is responsible for real-time source reconstruction {and, potentially, remote actuation.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: To achieve this, the \gls{bs employs a grant-free access mechanism to serve the monitoring device together with a broadband user, which share the available wireless resources through orthogonal or non-orthogonal multiple access schemes.}
elements.sent-2411-005: We analyse the performance of the system with time-averaged reconstruction error, time-averaged cost of actuation error, and update-delivery cost as performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we analyse the performance of the broadband user in terms of throughput and energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our results show that an orthogonal resource sharing between the users is beneficial in most cases where the broadband user requires maximum throughput.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, sharing the resources in a non-orthogonal manner leads to a far greater energy efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-009: Real-time autonomous and cyber-physical systems have attracted significant attention over the past decade due to their broad range of time-critical applications such as autonomous transportation and industrial automation~\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC,WEIMER2012178 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: These systems have devices, often geographically distributed, monitoring an information source or a process and sending updates to a remote monitor and/or digital twin.
elements.sent-2411-011: The objective of such systems is real-time tracking and reconstruction of the source process at the monitor receiving the updates, which enables decision-making and actuation~\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Several metrics, such as mean square estimation error, \gls{aoi and its variants, etc., have been studied as performance measures for remote tracking\cite{WEIMER2012178,Sun@AoI .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These metrics, however, are oblivious to the semantics of information (i.e., significance, goal-oriented usefulness, and contextual value) and its impact on the overall cyber-physical system\cite{TimePerspective@Popovski,Pappas@SmeanticsMagazine,pappas@dtmcTWC .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address this problem, recent works introduced metrics such as real-time reconstruction error and cost of actuation error, which account for the semantics of information transmitted and capture different aspects of the system performance\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC,pappas@dtmc .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.64\columnwidth, height=3.9cm]{Uplink_Process_Monitoring.pdf} \caption{A representative uplink scenario with a \gls{bs} serving an intermittent user and a broadband user.} \label{fig:BBU_IU_Scenrio}\glsreset{bs}
elements.sent-2411-015: Moreover, these often rely on the `idealistic' assumptions of always having wireless resources available for transmitting updates and instantaneous feedback\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, such assumptions are unrealistic, as these would require an exorbitant wastage of radio resources, particularly given the intermittent nature of device transmissions in real-time autonomous systems\cite{Ley23Asilomar .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, major challenges in wireless communication systems include the long access delay and overhead in grant-based access mechanisms, the inefficient radio resource utilization in grant-free access mechanisms, and the delayed transmission of the update or feedback in frame-based transmissions \cite{Ley23Asilomar,OJCOMMS@fedrico .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The massive number of heterogeneous devices in future wireless networks, such as \gls{6g , is going to exacerbate these challenges further.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, the analysis must account for relevant performance metrics, the user distribution across service types, and the multiple access mechanisms that allocate resources to each service ~\cite{OJCOMMS@fedrico,Chiariotti2021,pappas@dtmc .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Due to its intermittent behaviour, we consider that the monitoring device transmits its updates to the cellular network through a grant-free access mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-021: For ease of exposition, we will henceforth refer to this device as the intermittent user.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, a frame-based communication model is considered, where uplink and downlink transmissions are multiplexed in time.
elements.sent-2411-023: For the considered model, we analyse the performance metrics of the intermittent user and compare them with those obtained from the model employed in conventional studies on \textcolor{black {real-time remote tracking .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Here, we define \gls{udc as the average number of re-transmissions needed to successfully deliver an update.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We show that the desirably low \gls{tare and \gls{tacae performance of the communication model in conventional studies is achieved at the expense of a disproportionately high \gls{udc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We then study performance trade-offs between the intermittent user and the broadband user, whose metrics are throughput and energy efficiency, by sharing the available wireless resources using \gls{fdma and \gls{noma schemes.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: We consider an uplink scenario wherein a broadband user and an intermittent user are allocated wireless resources to communicate with the \gls{bs .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The users and the \gls{bs are equipped with a single antenna.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The users are indexed by $m\in\{1,2\}$, with the broadband user indexed as $m=1$ and the intermittent user as $m=2$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The available wireless resources are within a frequency band of bandwidth $B$ Hz\cite{Ley23Asilomar .}
elements.sent-2411-031: This bandwidth $B$ is divided into three sub-bands: $1$) $B_{1}$ reserved for the broadband user, $2$) $B_{2}$ reserved for the intermittent user, and $3$) $B_{3}$ to be shared by both users, such that $B_{1 +B_{2 +B_{3 =B$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Let $\alpha_{m,i}\in\{0,1\}$ indicate the allocation of user $m$ to sub-band $i\in\{1,2,3\}$, such that $\alpha_{m,i =1$ if user $m$ is assigned to sub-band $i$, and $\alpha_{m,i =0$ otherwise.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We consider a time-slotted communication system with $T_{s}$ as the duration of each slot.
elements.sent-2411-034: The communication is segmented into frames, with each frame consisting of $T_{F}$ consecutive time slots.
elements.sent-2411-035: Subsequently, we detail the multiple access schemes as follows.\footnote{\textcolor{black {We exclude \gls{tdma because it either restricts the choice of the sampling policy or renders the analysis intractable, requiring joint design of both the sampling policy and resource allocation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Hence, signal overlap between the users is avoided by setting $\alpha_{1,1 =\alpha_{2,2 =1$, $B=B_{1 +B_{2}$, and $B_{3 =0$\cite{Ley23Asilomar .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: This leads to a complete signal overlap between the users when both transmit in the same time slot\cite{Ley23Asilomar .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The first $T_{F -1$ slots are reserved for uplink transmission, while the last slot is reserved for downlink transmission to provide feedback, \gls{ack or \gls{nack , to the users from the \gls{bs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Here, the time slots and frames are indexed by $t\in\{0,1,\ldots \}$ and $f\in\{0,1,\ldots \}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-040: Fig.~\ref{fig:frame_access_mechanisms illustrates the frame structure with \gls{fdma and \gls{noma schemes\cite{Ley23Asilomar .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The user segments its transmit data into packets and employs a forward error correction (FEC) mechanism using an ideal rate-less packet-level coding, wherein blocks of $K$ source packets are encoded into blocks of linearly independent packets.
elements.sent-2411-042: Subsequently, the \gls{bs is able to decode a block of source packets once it has successfully received $K$ encoded packets representing the source block.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Successful/failed decoding of a source block is informed to the user by the \gls{bs using feedback at the end of a frame.}
elements.sent-2411-044: If the user receives an \gls{ack through feedback, then the user proceeds to transmit encoded packets of the next source block, otherwise, the user keeps transmitting the encoded packets of the current source block.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Such transmission strategy ensures that the broadband user is guaranteed to achieve reliability $1$.
elements.sent-2411-046: We model the information source at the user by an ergodic two-state \gls{dtmc $\{X_{t ,\,t\in\mathbb{Z _{0 ^{+}\}$, where the state $X_{t}$ of the source at time slot $t$ can be $0$ or $1$~\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The self-transition probabilities for states $0$ and $1$ are denoted as $1-p_{s}$ and $1-q_{s}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-048: As a result, $\mathbb{P}\left(X_{t+1 =X_{t}\right) = \mathbbm{1}\left(X_{t =0\right)\left(1-p_{s}\right)+\mathbbm{1}\left(X_{t =1\right)\left(1-q_{s}\right)$, with $\mathbbm{1 (\cdot)$ denoting the indicator function.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The user observes the process $X_{t}$ and informs the \gls{bs about its state by sending updates over a wireless channel.}
elements.sent-2411-050: To that end, the user will generate an update $X_{t}$ through sampling, and transmit the update to the \gls{bs in a data packet of length $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Moreover, we assume that the intermittent user has a transmission queue of length 1, so any new update replaces the previous packet in the queue.
elements.sent-2411-052: The actions of sampling and transmission at time slot $t$ are denoted by $\alpha_{t ^{s}\in \{0,1\}$ and $\alpha_{t ^{tx}\in \{0,1\}$, respectively, where $\alpha_{t ^{a =1$, $\,a\in \{s,tx\}$, indicates that the action is taken at time slot $t$, and $\alpha_{t ^{a =0$ otherwise.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Next, if the \gls{bs successfully receives the packet, it updates the state of the reconstructed source instantaneously.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Notwithstanding, if the sampling policy requires feedback and multiple updates are sent within a frame, feedback will be provided only for the latest update.
elements.sent-2411-055: Fig.~\ref{fig:dtmc illustrates the process monitoring of the considered source process.}
elements.sent-2411-056: As highlighted in Section \ref{Intro_Section , the reconstructed process at the \gls{bs can be utilized to construct a digital twin or effectuate real-time actuation.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We denote the channel coefficient between the \gls{bs and user $m$ at time slot $t$ as $h_{m,t}\in \mathbb{C}$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The channel coefficient is modeled as a random variable that accounts for large-scale and small-scale fading losses.
elements.sent-2411-059: The small-scale fading loss is modeled as a \gls{cscg random variable with zero mean and unit variance.}
elements.sent-2411-060: On the other hand, the large-scale fading loss is a function of the speed of light $c$, the carrier frequency $f_{c}$, the distance between the \gls{bs and user $m$, $d_{m}$, the path loss exponent $\eta$, and the antenna gains at the \gls{bs and the user, $G_{b}$ and $G_{m}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, the large-scale fading loss, $\beta_{m}$, can be computed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:LSF} \beta_{m}=\mathbb{E}\{\lvert h_{m,t}\rvert^{2}\}=\frac{G_{m}G_{b}c^{2}}{\left(4\pi f_{c}\right)^{2}d_{m}^{\eta}},\:m\in\{1,2\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: By $x_{m,t}\in\mathbb{C}$ we denote as the transmit signal between user $m$ and the \gls{bs at time slot $t$, and by $P_{m,t}\in [0,P_{max ]$ as the transmission power of user $m$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Subsequently, the received signal at the \gls{bs during uplink transmission in the $i$-th sub-band and $t$-th time slot can be written as \begin{equation}\label{eq:rx_sig} y_{i,t}= h_{1,t}x_{1,t}\alpha_{1,i} +h_{2,t}x_{2,t}\alpha_{2,i} + w_{i,t},\; \forall i\in\{1,2,3\},\end{equation} where $w_{i,t}$ is the \gls{cscg distributed additive noise with zero mean and variance $\sigma_{i ^{2 =B_{i}\kappa T_{w 10^{N_{f /10}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Here, $\kappa$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T_{w}$ is the system noise temperature, and $N_{f}$ is the noise figure in dB.
elements.sent-2411-065: Hence, the \gls{sinr for the signal of user $m$ in the $i$-th sub-band and $t$-th time slot can be computed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:sinr_mit} \gamma_{m,i,t}=\frac{\lvert h_{m,t}\rvert^{2}P_{m,t}\alpha_{m,i}}{\lvert h_{n,t}\rvert^{2}P_{n,t}\alpha_{n,i}+\sigma_{i}^{2}},\,\, m,n\in\{1,2\}\,\wedge\,m\neq n.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Assuming the interference can be treated as Gaussian noise, we define a threshold for decoding the signal of user $m$ at time slot $t$ in sub-band $i$ as the function of the rate $r_{m}$, as \begin{equation}\label{eq:sinr_min} \gamma_{m,i}^{min}\left(r_{m}\right)=2^{r_{m}/B_{i}}-1,\end{equation} such that if $\gamma_{m,i,t}\geq \gamma_{m,i ^{min}\left(r_{m}\right)$, then the packet of user $m$ is successfully decoded at time slot $t$; we denote the corresponding probability of successful decoding by \begin{equation}\label{eq:success_prob} p_{m,i,t}=\mathbb{P}\left(\gamma_{m,i,t}\geq \gamma_{m,i}^{min}\left(r_{m}\right)\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Further, we introduce the error probability for user $m$ as $\epsilon_{m,i}$, defining it as the probability of a transmission error in the absence of interference from the other user $n\neq m$, given by \begin{IEEEeqnarray {C \epsilon_{m,i = \mathbb{P}\left(\gamma_{m,i,t}\leq \gamma_{m,i ^{min}\left(r_{m}\right)\!:\alpha_{m,i =1\wedge P_{n,t}\alpha_{n,i =0\right)\!.\IEEEeqnarraynumspace \end{IEEEeqnarray {The \gls{bs aims to decode the received signals instantaneously, i.e., in the same time slot it received the packet.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: While decoding the signal of user $m$ in the \gls{fdma regime is straightforward, we consider a \gls{sic decoder in conjunction with the capture effect for \gls{noma}\cite{OJCOMMS@fedrico,Ley23Asilomar .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The success or failure of the decoding process is dictated by the \gls{sinr defined in \eqref{eq:sinr_mit .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we derive the expression for metrics relevant to the performance of the broadband and intermittent user.
elements.sent-2411-071: For the broadband user, we consider throughput and energy efficiency to be the performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-072: To this end, the transmission rate $r_{1}$ is selected as the minimum of: 1) the maximum rate $r_{1 ^{max}$ and 2) the maximum achievable data rate for the target error probability $\epsilon_{1 ^{*}$, expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:BBuser_r1} r_{1}=\max\{r\in (0,r_{1}^{max}]:\epsilon_{1,i}\left(r\right)=\epsilon_{1}^{*}, P_{1,t}\leq P_{m}^{max}\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: For the desired rate $r_{1}$ and the target error probability $\epsilon_{1 ^{*}$, $P_{1,t}$ can be calculated by assuming absence of interference in \eqref{eq:sinr_mit and then utilizing equations \eqref{eq:erasure_prob and \eqref{eq:success_prob , given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Power_BBUser} P_{1,t}=\min\left(\frac{\left(2^{r_{1}/B_{i}}-1\right)\sigma_{i}^{2}}{\mathbb{E}[|h_{1}|^{2}]\log(\epsilon_{1}^{*}-1)},P_{max}\right),\end{equation} where it is assumed that the broadband user has statistical knowledge of its channel, i.e., of $\mathbb{E [|h_{1 |^{2 ]$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Denoting the random variable of the number of frames needed to decode the block of $K$ source packets transmitted by the user as $F(K)$, the throughput of the broadband user is calculated as \begin{equation}\label{eq:BBuser_Throughput} S_{B}=r_{i}K\left(T_{F}-1\right)/\left(\mathbb{E}\{F(K)\}T_{F}\right),\end{equation} and the energy efficiency is calculated as $S_{B /P_{1,t}$.}
elements.sent-2411-075: We consider context-dependent and cost-aware performance metrics for the intermittent user \cite{pappas@dtmcTWC,pappas@dtmc .}
elements.sent-2411-076: These rely on the \gls{bs constructing an estimate of the original source state $X_{t}$ at time slot $t$, denoted by $\widehat{X _{t}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: These metrics are described in the following.
elements.sent-2411-078: The real-time reconstruction error is calculated as $E_{t =\mathbbm{1}\left(X_{t}\neq\widehat{X _{t}\right)\in\{0,1\}$\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dtmc , $E_{t}$ can be construed as an indicator for the state of the system, with $E_{t =1$ being the erroneous state and $E_{t =0$ being the synced state.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Subsequently, for an observation interval of $[1,T_{o ]$, with $T_{o}$ being a large number, the \gls{tare is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Iuser_ARE} \bar{E}=\lim_{T_{o}\rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{T_{o}}\sum_{t=1}^{T_{o}}{E}_{t}= \frac{1}{T_{o}}\sum_{t=1}^{T_{o}}\mathbbm{1}\left(X_{t}\neq\widehat{X}_{t}\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Interestingly, the evolution of $E_{t}$ can be described by a Markov chain as well, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:dtmc ; with transition probabilities defined as $p_{ji ^{E =\mathbb{P}\left(E_{t+1 =j\mid E_{t =i\right),\, \forall i,j\in\{0,1\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: For ease of understanding, we derive the general expression of the transition probabilities $p_{00 ^{E}$ and $p_{11 ^{E}$ as follows. \begin{equation}\label{eq:Iuser_REP00} p_{00}^{E}=\sum_{i=0}^{1} \mathbb{P}\left(E_{t+1}=0\mid E_{t}=0,\,X_{t}=i\right)\mathbb{P}\left(X_{t}=i\right),\end{equation} with $\mathbb{P}\left(X_{t =i\right)=\mathbbm{1 (i=0)\frac{q_{s {p_{s +q_{s +\mathbbm{1 (i=1)\frac{p_{s {p_{s +q_{s ,\,\forall i\in\{0,1\}$, calculated using stationary distribution of $X_{t}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: To compute \eqref{eq:Iuser_REP00 , we first define an indicator event for successful decoding of a transmitted packet of the intermittent user as $\alpha^{d}$ such that $p_{2,i,t =\mathbb{P}\left(\alpha_{t ^{d =1\right)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Next, with $j\in \{0,1\}$, we denote probabilities of joint sampling, transmission, and decoding events at time $t+1$ as $p_{stx ^{j =\mathbb{P}\left(\alpha_{t+1 ^{s =1,\alpha_{t+1 ^{tx =j\right)$, and $p_{stx ^{d_{j =\mathbb{P}\left(\alpha_{t+1 ^{s =1, \alpha_{t+1 ^{tx =1, \alpha_{t+1 ^{d =j\right)$; Section \ref{SysModTransMod_IU delineates events $a_{t ^{s}$ and $a_{t ^{tx}$ in detail.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In regard to $p_{11 ^{E}$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:Iuser_REP110} \mathbb{P}\left(E_{t+1}=1\mid E_{t}=1,X_{t}=0\right) =(1-p_{s})(p_{stx}^{0}+p_{stx}^{d_{0}}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-089: To obtain $\mathbb{P}\left(E_{t+1 =1\mid E_{t =1,\,X_{t =1\right)$, we simply replace $p_{s}$ in \eqref{eq:Iuser_REP110 by $q_{s}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Evidently, the transition probabilities depend on the sampling policy as well as the multiple access scheme.
elements.sent-2411-091: Moreover, we assume $C_{i,j}\neq C_{j,i}$ as different errors have different repercussions on the actuation.
elements.sent-2411-092: Finally, we assume that $C_{i,j}$, $\forall i,j$, is static over the entire observation interval.
elements.sent-2411-093: Following the above discussion, we calculate the \gls{tacae as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Iuser_AE} \bar{C}_{A}= \pi_{\left(0,1\right)}C_{0,1} + \pi_{\left(1,0\right)}C_{1,0},\end{equation} where $\pi_{\left(i,j\right) ,\,\forall i,j\in\{0,1\}$ is obtained from the stationary distribution of the \gls{dtmc , describing the joint status of the source state $X_{t}$ and the reconstruction error state $E_{t}$ at $t$.}}}
elements.section-2411-004: Sampling Policy and Multiple Access Schemes
elements.sent-2411-094: In this section we delineate the sampling policy for process monitoring at the intermittent user and highlight its effect on the performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-095: Keeping this in mind, we consider the semantics-aware sampling policy which triggers sample generation in two cases\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}
elements.sent-2411-096: First, whenever a change in the state of the source between two consecutive slots is observed, i.e., ${X _{t+1}\neq{X _{t}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Second, when the system is known to be in erroneous state, i.e., $\widehat{X _{t}\neq {X _{t}$\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Such a policy integrates well with \gls{fdma and \gls{noma .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover, unlike uniform and change-aware sampling policies, the semantics-aware policy requires feedback from the receiver to the transmitter to trigger sample generation\cite{pappas@dtmcTWC .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, feedback is used to re-transmit failed update deliveries.
elements.sent-2411-101: The sampling policy, together with the frame structure, dictates the probability of sampling and transmission events and in turn the value of $p_{stx ^{j}$ in equations \eqref{eq:Iuser_REP000 and \eqref{eq:Iuser_REP110 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: On the other hand, together with the choice of multiple access scheme, which dictates the probability of successful decoding $p_{2,i,t}$, it determines the value of $p_{stx ^{d_{j }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: To this end, we delineate the calculation of $p_{2,i,t}$ for \gls{fdma and \gls{noma schemes.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We assume the intermittent user has no knowledge of its channel due to its sporadic access behavior and, therefore, always transmits at power $P_{max}$.
elements.sent-2411-105: As the users are allocated orthogonal sub-bands, the signal of the intermittent user is not subject to interference from the broadband user.
elements.sent-2411-106: Therefore, $p_{2,i,t}$ for the transmitted packet at time slot $t$ can be calculated from \eqref{eq:success_prob , where $\gamma_{2,i,t}$, obtained using \eqref{eq:sinr_mit , will be devoid of the interference term.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Since users share time-frequency resources in \gls{noma and the capture effect can occur, the successful decoding probabilities of both users' signals at time slot $t$ become interdependent.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Therefore, to calculate $p_{i,2,t}$ for the intermittent user, we first define two possible outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-109: Either $1$) the signal of the intended user has a sufficiently high \gls{sinr to be decoded directly, or $2$) the signal from the other user is decoded first, its interference is eliminated using \gls{sic , and then the intended signal has a sufficiently high \gls{snr and gets decoded.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Subsequently, the probability of successful decoding of the signal of the intermittent user can be calculated as $p_{2,i,t =\pi_{\mathcal{I}\mathcal{I +\pi_{\mathcal{E}\mathcal{I }$, where both $\pi_{\mathcal{I}\mathcal{I }$ and $\pi_{\mathcal{E}\mathcal{I }$ can be obtained from Appendix A in \cite{OJCOMMS@fedrico .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: In this section, we evaluate the performance of the broadband user and the intermittent user sharing resources in the considered frame-based communication model.
elements.sent-2411-112: Here, the broadband user is located at a distance of $50\,$m, whereas the intermittent user is located at a distance $d\,$ from the \gls{bs .}
elements.sent-2411-113: The system parameters are listed in Table \ref{tab:Sim_table .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Simulation parameters} \label{tab:Sim_table} \begin{tabular}{l l l} [0.4mm] Parameter Symbol Value [0.4mm] Broadband user erasure probability $\epsilon^{*}$ $0.1$ Broadband user maximum data rate $r_{1}^{max}$ $5$ Mbps Maximum transmission power $P_{max}$ $200$ mW Antenna gains $G_{t}G_{r}$ $10$ Time slot duration $T_{s}$ $1$ ms Carrier frequency $f_{c}$ $2$ GHz System bandwidth $B$ $1$ MHz Noise temperature $T_{w}$ $190$ K Noise figure $N_{f}$ $5$ dB Frame length $T_{F}$ $10$ Broadband user source block length $K$ $32$ Intermittent user packet length $L$ $128\,B$ \gls{bs}-intermittent user distance $d$ $\{100, 200, 400\}\,$m Path loss exponent $\eta$ $2.6$ Cost of actuation error coefficients $C_{i,j}$ $C_{0,1}=5,\,C_{1,0}=1$ [0.4mm] \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-114: To begin with, we analyse the performance of the intermittent user for \gls{fdma frames (i.e., $B_{3 =0$), with increasing value of $B_{2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Such model `idealistically' assumes instantaneous transmission of the updates and feedback, hence, is denoted as the `Idealistic' model throughout this section.
elements.sent-2411-116: The time-averaged reconstruction error, \gls{tare , for the Idealistic model with the semantics-aware sampling policy can be analytically calculated as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Iuser_RE_Theo} \bar{E}=\frac{2\,p_{s}q_{s}\left(1-p_{2,i,t}\right)}{p_{2,i,t}\left(p_{s}+q_{s}\right)+4p_{s}q_{s}\left(1-p_{2,i,t}\right)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-117: However, deriving the analytical expression of the \gls{tare for the model in Section \ref{SysMod_TransMod , denoted as the `Frame-Based' model, is only tractable for sampling policies that do not require feedback, e.g., uniform and change-aware.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This is due to the constraints of the frame structure on the transmission of feedback, discussed in Section \ref{Sys_Mod .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Since the considered semantics-aware sampling policy requires feedback, deriving an analytical expression similar to \eqref{eq:Iuser_RE_Theo for the Frame-Based model becomes intractable.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Therefore, we numerically evaluate the performance of the intermittent user in the Frame-Based model by simulating more than $100000$ frames.
elements.sent-2411-121: The relatively small values of $p_s$ and $q_s$ imply that the \gls{dtmc source is changing slowly, whereas varying values of $d$ are considered to observe the \gls{tare for varying decoding probabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The figure illustrates that the successful decoding probability increases with increasing $B_{2}$ and, consequently, the \gls{tare at the \gls{bs decreases.}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Moreover, the farther the intermittent user is from the \gls{bs , the larger $B_{2}$ needs to be to reduce the \gls{tare .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: As expected, with increasing $B_{2}$ the decline in \gls{tare is steeper for the Idealistic model.}
elements.sent-2411-125: This gets more pronounced with increasing $d$.
elements.sent-2411-126: However, the performance gain of the Idealistic model over the Frame-Based model comes at the expense of very high \gls{udc , as illustrated in Table \ref{tab:CAE_RE_SC for parameters $B_{2 =0.4\,B$ and $d=400$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: The table shows that while \gls{tare for the Idealistic model is $70\%$ less than that in Frame-Based model, the \gls {udc is $134\%$ more.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Similar behaviour is observed with the \gls{tacae metric; albeit the difference in performance is narrower, as observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:ReconstructionError_IU}$\left(b\right)$.}
elements.sent-2411-129: It can be observed that as the \gls{dtmc process becomes fast changing, both error metrics increase for the Idealistic model.}
elements.sent-2411-130: In comparison, for the Frame-Based model, the \gls{tare remains the same and the \gls{tacae decreases.}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Such discrepancy is by virtue of the fact that delayed feedback is the limiting factor for the Frame-Based model.
elements.sent-2411-132: Therefore, as the process becomes fast changing, it leads to more frequent updates which in turn mitigates the inimical impact of delayed feedback.
elements.sent-2411-133: Alternatively, the results also suggest that the desired error-metric performance for the Frame-Based model can be achieved by adjusting the frame length, with only a modest change in the \gls{udc .}

elements.sent-2411-148: A communication model predicated on `idealistic' assumptions in conventional studies on \textcolor{black {real-time remote reconstruction was considered as the baseline.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: We demonstrated that the error-metrics performance gain of the baseline model in conventional systems over Frame-Based model is achieved at the expense of a disproportionately high update-delivery cost.
elements.sent-2411-150: After that, for the Frame-Based model, we analysed the performance trade-off between the intermittent user effectuating the real-time remote tracking and a broadband user, for \gls{fdma and \gls{noma schemes.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Building on this work, future studies will investigate joint optimization of the sampling policy and the uplink transmit power to improve the performance metrics of both users.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
